mnemonics.title: WHERE vs HAVING
mnemonics.content: WHERE filters 'raw' rows, HAVING filters 'cooked' groups - like filtering flour before vs after baking

mnemonics.title: IN vs EXISTS
mnemonics.content: IN is for 'membership check', EXISTS is for 'existence check' - like checking if someone is in a list vs checking if anyone is home

mnemonics.title: Join Types
mnemonics.content: LEFT keeps LEFT table's rows, RIGHT keeps RIGHT table's rows, FULL keeps BOTH, INNER keeps ONLY matches

acronyms.usage: Temporary named result set within query scope, improves readability
SQL commands that manipulate data: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
Represents missing or unknown data; NULL is not zero or empty string
Standard language for managing relational databases

visualTips.visualization: Think of nested boxes - outer query holds inner query, inner query resolves first and passes result to outer
Venn diagrams - INNER: overlap only, LEFT: left circle + overlap, RIGHT: right circle + overlap, FULL: both circles + overlap, CROSS: entire rectangle
For each row in outer table, inner query runs again - like checking each student's marks against class average

analogies.analogy: Like checking each student's result against their class average - you need to know the student (outer row) first, then calculate average for their class (inner query)
Like writing a rough draft before final essay - define intermediate result once, use multiple times in the same query
WHERE is like filtering vegetables before cooking, HAVING is like tasting and adjusting seasoning after the dish is prepared
